description.value:
Source: Ariadne's Book of Legends - Alpha v1.2, p. 120-122.

The Great Arcanisms

L

egendary Spellcasters and Legendary Foes alike can access 10th-level spells, arcanisms tantamount to the most powerful effects that can occur in your game– particularly in combat. Only ancient rituals of great cost or direct divine mandates can surpass them, and even then, 10th-level spells remain the building blocks of those realitybending workings. They are just about the hardest thing a mortal can learn.

Epic Magic

The scale of 10th-level spells is beyond that of any nonLegendary effect. As noted in the Magic section of the introduction, Epic Magic encompasses any effect that surpasses 9th level–divine authority, Wish-like workings, and, of course, 10th-level spells. Epic Magic is the only thing that can withstand or alter Epic Magic.

Many effects you will encounter in the following pages are singular, well-defined 10th-level arcanisms. In your Legendary Campaign, however, you may come across a variety of Epic Magic effects that are broader or less certain. A demigod’s gift of invulnerability, a divine dragon’s unique breath weapon, a void creature’s ability to fracture reality with each strike–even the powerful effects of a Relic-level magical item! The DM determines what qualifies as Epic Magic, and therefore what can only be interacted with, countered, or replaced by an equally powerful Epic effect. Non-Legendary magic–even effects as sweeping as Antimagic Field– cannot stop Epic Magic.

Clashes of Epic Magic What happens when two divine heralds clash in their proclamations? What happens when you cast counterspell at 10th level to stop an enemy from casting a 10th-level spell? In these situations, the DM has considerable latitude in making a ruling, but we suggest erring on the players’ side and using contests and rolls to settle complex interactions. Shutting down your player’s Miracle with a 10th-level counterspell is brutally anticlimactic. You can ask yourself the following questions to reach a sound ruling between “it just works”, “it doesn’t work”, and “it may work, with a roll”.

Which caster is mightier or more skilled in magic? +

Is the caster of the challenged effect present to try and overcome the challenge, or is the magical effect standing on its own?

What would allowing a challenge to this effect imply in worldbuilding terms? +

And, perhaps most important of all, how would your ruling affect the fun of the table? Both you and your players are here for a good time, not realism.

The Cost of Epic Magic

Casting a 10th-level spell requires an action, a 10thlevel spell slot, and 2 Legendary Points. This is an extraordinary investment of resources in the middle of combat. When executed properly, Epic Magic can have such an effect as to halt a battle in its tracks.

These great arcanisms become available at 22nd level, and spellcasters learn at least one additional 10thlevel spell before their Legendary Adventure is over. A great part of a spellcaster’s power budget is baked into these effects.

The 10th-Level Spell Slot

The 10th-level spell slot introduced in this expansion also allows existing spells to be upcast to a higher level. Upcasting with a 10th-level slot, however, is a categorically different matter from a standard upcast. To use a 10th-level spell slot this way, you must still expend 2 Legendary Points. A spell upcast at 10th level gains additional features–less powerful than those of a true 10th-level spell, but greater than a standard upcast. This is a great way of creating your own 10th-level spells!

When a spell is upcast using a 10th-level spell slot, it can gain one or more of the following enhancements, at the DM’s discretion:

Greater Scale. The spell’s area of effect, range, or number of targets increases significantly beyond its standard upcast progression. A 10th-level Meteor Swarm may bring down enough comets to raze a city, rather than just a small district.

Heightened Potency. The spell’s damage, healing, or primary effect increases beyond its standard upcast progression. A 10th-level Greater Restoration may end all the harmful effects listed in its description, rather than just one of them.

Lingering Effect. The spell leaves a persistent consequence in its wake–a zone of altered terrain, a lasting condition, or a secondary effect that continues long after the spell ends. A 10th-level Time Stop may leave a small zone with permanent disruptions in the flow of time, such as a loop or accelerated/slowed time.

Unendurable. The spell ignores resistances and treats immunities as resistances. A 10th-level Fireball may harm even fire elementals, so arcanely suffused the spell becomes.

Unavoidable. Creatures that succeed on their saving throw against the spell still suffer a reduced version of its effect, rather than none at all. A 10thlevel Disintegrate may still deal half damage to a creature that succeeded on the save.

Material Components

10th-level spells require material components of incredible value. Unless those components are consumed upon use, they are mostly flavorful additions to the mechanics of the game; as the DM, it is up to you whether these are required or a focus can do the trick.

Magic in your Game

D&D 5e / 5.5e has some difficulties with certain highlevel spells. In a Legendary Campaign, where 9th-level spells are plentiful and often go unused between long rests, a character might choose to cast Wish, dubbed the “mightiest spell a mortal can cast”, every night until they are unable to do so anymore. This can quickly unravel your game if you aren’t prepared.

There are some fixes that can be proposed for certain spells, though a Legendary Campaign can very well contend with these effects as written. Legendary Foes have the tools not to be overwhelmed by unusual spell combinations accumulated over a long campaign.

That said, the following spells deserve special attention at your table. Each entry describes why the spell may become problematic at Legendary levels, and offers a fix you can adopt. As the DM, feel free to take as many or as few as you’d like into your table, and communicate clearly with your players about how these effects have changed. As a player, be patient with your DM! They are trying to make this fun for everyone without non-legendary legacy rules getting in the way of the narrative.

Wish. Is it really the mightiest spell a mortal can cast, if there are 10th-level spells in the roster? That aside, with the legendary level progression, it becomes realistic for a caster to use it two or even three times a day, depending on their class. Hardly the epic, one-of-a-kind arcanism it was in non-legendary play…

Our Fix: 9th-level Wish is now called Lesser Wish and has greater limitations, allowing only to create the stated effects, or similarly powerful ones–not being considered Epic Magic. 10th-level Wish, called True Wish, expands on those effects and is actually the mightiest spell a mortal can cast. More about these changes on the True Wish spell!

Simulacrum. At Legendary levels, a duplicate of a 22nd-level spellcaster is a second legendary character at the table, quickly breaking the intended balance of legendary encounters.

Our Fix: A Simulacrum created at Legendary levels remains at 20th-level and doesn’t have access to Legendary Points, resistances, and other legendary features.

Forcecage and other elements of force. An inescapable cage that blocks teleportation is already one of the most frustrating spells in the game at the 7thlevel category. Against a Legendary Foe with no reliable saving throw bonus against Dexterity, it can trivialize encounters entirely.

Our Fix: Legendary Foes and Legendary Characters treat Forcecage as a contested effect they can break out of using their strongest ability score. When they end their turn trapped in a construction of magical force, they make a DC 30 ability saving throw of their choice. On a success, they break out of their confinement in a way consistent with their chosen ability: a Barbarian might break through the forcefield, tearing it apart with their bare hands, while a Wizard may find a weakness in the arcane matrix of the wall, destabilizing it with a precisely calculated burst of prana. The construction of magical force closes behind the character after their escape. This can also be used in other cases of magical confinement of a Legendary Creature, as long as that magical confinement comes from a non-Legendary effect, a Legendary Foe or Character will always be able to escape it. Additionally, we recommend letting mythical techniques and Artifact and Relic weapons destroy constructions of magical force.

Shapechange and True Polymorph. The ability to become any creature with a Challenge Rating equal to your level is already extraordinarily broad at 17th level. At 22nd, it can give access to stat blocks that dwarf what any encounter was designed to handle.

Our Fix: Shapechange and True Polymorph have a CR limit of 20.

Teleport, Plane Shift, and Gate. Escape spells are a natural part of 5e, and in most campaigns, a reasonable one. In a Legendary Campaign where the DM has invested in complex, multi-session scenarios, a single Teleport can bypass entire narrative arcs.

Our Fix: Create more zones that don’t allow for teleporting in or out of them with a non-Epic effect! Maintain the ability to teleport within the boss arena, for example, but close the planar boundaries around it, ensuring there is no chance of running away for either your players or your boss monster, or at least not without significant resource expenditure.

Magical Rulings As a DM, you will probably make a few questionable rulings, especially when it comes to how certain magical effects interact. That’s okay. Don’t be afraid of changing things up if they are clearly not working as intended. Use your gut! Perhaps you find that some spell is having too much impact during a battle; don’t let that spell trivialize an encounter because of a technicality.

Also, keep in mind the Tiers of Magic rule from the introduction, which allows you to determine that a certain magical effect or feature does not come into play when measured against greater magic; perhaps you determine that Death Ward (4th-level), powerful as it is, is completely bypassed by the 10th-level spell Killing Winds, even if that is not specifically written out. It’s your call!

Epic Spells

The following is a list of all Epic Spells available to Legendary Characters within this supplement. Epic Spells belong to the eight established schools of magic and are segmented by class. Take the time to evaluate all your options whenever you gain access to a 10thlevel spell, and create a plausible flavor-like explanation for your learning of a certain arcanism. This is your ultimate power-up–how did you come across it?

LP Cost. As a Legendary Character, casting a 10thlevel spell requires 2 Legendary Points, an action, and the appropriate spell slot. Legendary Foes play by different rules – with multiple actions and recharging LP, casting a 10th-level spell is often a multi-round endeavor and a costly matter, giving the party a chance to react before the great arcanisms are unleashed upon them.

Epic Spells
